guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 41 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: New Year's Day
festivals.description: Thailand's official New Year celebration marking the start of the calendar year with government and business closures nationwide.

festivals.name: Phanom Rung Festival
festivals.description: A spectacular light and sound show at the ancient Khmer temple of Phanom Rung, celebrating the spring equinox with cultural performances. The sun perfectly aligns with the temple's central chamber during sunrise.

festivals.name: Magha Bucha
festivals.description: A major Buddhist holiday commemorating the day 1,250 enlightened disciples spontaneously visited Buddha, marked by candlelit processions around temples.

festivals.name: Chiang Mai Flower Festival
festivals.description: Thailand's most vibrant flower celebration featuring massive floral floats, parades, and displays showcasing the region's abundant blooms. The city transforms into a garden of color and fragrance.

festivals.name: Makha Bucha Full Moon
festivals.description: Buddhist religious observance with temple visits, merit-making, and candlelit circumambulations around temple grounds nationwide.

festivals.name: Pattaya Festival
festivals.description: A week-long celebration featuring parades, cultural performances, water sports competitions, and fireworks celebrating Pattaya's founding and culture.

festivals.name: Songkran Festival
festivals.description: Thailand's most exuberant celebration marking the traditional New Year with massive water-splashing festivities, temple visits, and family gatherings. Streets become playgrounds for joyful water battles.

festivals.name: Rocket Festival (Bun Bangfai)
festivals.description: A uniquely northeastern celebration where villages compete launching homemade bamboo rockets into the sky to invoke rain for the rice-planting season. The festival combines spirituality with raucous fun.

festivals.name: Visakha Bucha
festivals.description: Thailand's holiest Buddhist holiday commemorating Buddha's birth, enlightenment, and nirvana with nationwide temple ceremonies and candlelit processions.

festivals.name: Chiang Mai City Arts and Culture Festival
festivals.description: A cultural showcase celebrating northern traditions through art exhibitions, performances, and parades showcasing local craftsmanship and heritage.

festivals.name: International Dance and Music Festival
festivals.description: Bangkok hosts international and Thai performers in an eclectic celebration of world dance and music traditions.

festivals.name: Corpus Christi Festival
festivals.description: A Catholic religious celebration in Kanchanaburi, featuring processions and religious services commemorating the body of Christ.

festivals.name: Asalha Bucha
festivals.description: An important Buddhist holiday marking Buddha's first sermon, observed with temple rituals and merit-making across all of Thailand.

festivals.name: Buddhist Lent (Khao Phansa)
festivals.description: The beginning of a three-month Buddhist retreat period where monks remain in temples and laypeople observe increased religious devotion nationwide.

festivals.name: Lopburi Monkey Festival
festivals.description: A quirky celebration honoring the thousands of monkeys living in Lopburi's ancient temples with a massive fruit banquet served by locals. One of Thailand's most unique and entertaining festivals.

festivals.name: Nan Boat Races
festivals.description: Traditional long-boat racing competition on the Nan River celebrating the end of the monsoon season with colorful boats and energetic competitions.

festivals.name: Surin Elephant Roundup
festivals.description: Thailand's most famous elephant festival featuring hundreds of decorated elephants in parades, performances, and demonstrations showcasing these majestic animals.

festivals.name: Hua Hin Jazz Festival
festivals.description: An upscale beach festival bringing international jazz musicians to Thailand's royal seaside resort town with performances and cultural events.

festivals.name: Krabi Seafood Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of fresh seafood with cooking competitions, beach markets, and cultural performances highlighting the region's culinary treasures.

festivals.name: Phuket Old Town Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of Phuket's sino-Portuguese heritage featuring processions, street food stalls, and cultural performances in the historic old town.

festivals.name: Thai Floating Markets Season
festivals.description: The peak season to visit Thailand's iconic floating markets where vendors sell fresh produce, cooked food, and souvenirs from wooden boats in waterways.

festivals.name: King's Birthday
festivals.description: A major national holiday celebrating the late King Bhumibol's birthday with decorations, merit-making ceremonies, and official celebrations throughout Thailand.

festivals.name: New Year's Eve/Countdown
festivals.description: Major celebration in Bangkok and resort towns with massive countdowns, fireworks, street parties, and New Year festivities.

festivals.name: Thai Festival
festivals.description: Bangkok's largest annual cultural festival celebrating Thai heritage with traditional performances, crafts, food, and exhibitions across multiple venues.

festivals.name: Loy Krathong Festival
festivals.description: Thailand's most poetic festival where people float decorated baskets on water to pay respects and wash away negativity, creating stunning nighttime scenes.

festivals.name: Yi Peng Lantern Festival
festivals.description: Thousands of illuminated lanterns float into the night sky in Chiang Mai, creating one of the world's most magical and visually stunning celebrations.

festivals.name: Chiang Rai Night Bazaar Festival
festivals.description: A festive celebration of Chiang Rai's night bazaar with live performances, food vendors, and shopping featuring local crafts and products.

festivals.name: Phuket King's Cup Regatta
festivals.description: One of Asia's most prestigious sailing competitions held in Phuket with international yachts competing against a backdrop of beautiful Andaman waters.

festivals.name: Bangkok International Fashion Week
festivals.description: Thailand's premier fashion event showcasing collections from designers across Southeast Asia and the world in the heart of Bangkok.

festivals.name: Constitution Day
festivals.description: A national holiday commemorating Thailand's constitution with government ceremonies and reduced activities across the nation.

festivals.name: Labor Day
festivals.description: An international holiday honoring workers with reduced business activity and some ceremonial events across Thailand.

festivals.name: Pattaya International Music Festival
festivals.description: A beach-side music festival featuring international and Thai performers with concerts and cultural events in Pattaya.

festivals.name: Bangkok International Film Festival
festivals.description: Thailand's premier cinema event showcasing local and international films with screenings and industry events across Bangkok.

festivals.name: Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya Historical Park Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of Thailand's ancient capital with light shows, parades, and historical performances among the temple ruins.

festivals.name: Nakhon Pathom Chedi Festival
festivals.description: A celebration honoring the world's tallest Buddhist pagoda with traditional performances, merit-making ceremonies, and cultural events.

festivals.name: Tak Loy Krathong Phi Festival
festivals.description: A unique Loy Krathong celebration in Tak where ghost-themed floats and traditional baskets are released on the Ping River.

festivals.name: Phasin Fabric and Cultural Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of northeastern silk weaving tradition featuring exhibitions, demonstrations, and sales of the region's famous handwoven fabrics.

festivals.name: Chiang Khong Boat Festival
festivals.description: A river festival in the Mekong border town celebrating the end of monsoon with traditional long-boat races and cultural performances.

festivals.name: Songkhla Lake Festival
festivals.description: A festival celebrating southern Thailand's largest lagoon with boat races, seafood markets, and cultural performances.

festivals.name: Lampang Horse Cart Festival
festivals.description: A unique celebration of Lampang's horse-drawn carriage tradition featuring decorated carts parading through the city in colorful processions.

festivals.name: Sunkhla Loy Krathong
festivals.description: A southern regional variation of Loy Krathong featuring water activities, parades, and traditional merit-making ceremonies in coastal settings.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Thailand
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Hunter-Gatherers and Early Settlement
items.summary: Ancient peoples hunted, fished, and foraged across the Thai peninsula and river valleys.
items.text: Archaeological evidence from Ban Chiang and other sites reveals that humans inhabited present-day Thailand for millennia before agriculture took hold. These early inhabitants were skilled hunters who also gathered plants, fruits, and shellfish from the abundant environment. They gradually adapted to seasonal flooding patterns of major rivers like the Mekong and Chao Phraya, developing techniques suited to tropical monsoon ecology. Over time, some groups began experimenting with cultivation of rice and domestication of animals, laying the groundwork for settled agricultural societies.

items.label: Bronze Age Civilization
items.summary: Early agricultural societies flourished with bronze working and sophisticated pottery traditions.
items.text: Ban Chiang culture flourished during this period, producing distinctive pottery with red and black geometric designs and pioneering bronze metallurgy. These communities cultivated wet rice in river valleys, which supported larger, more permanent settlements than hunting-gathering societies could sustain. Bronze tools and weapons gave them advantages in farming and defense, while their trade networks extended across Southeast Asia. The social hierarchies evident in burial sites suggest emerging chieftaincies rather than egalitarian communities, marking a crucial step toward state formation.

items.label: Iron Age and Early Kingdoms
items.summary: Iron tools revolutionized agriculture while Indian cultural influences arrived via trade routes.
items.text: The introduction of iron technology dramatically increased agricultural productivity and population growth across the region. Meanwhile, Indian merchants and missionaries brought Theravada Buddhism, Sanskrit, and Hindu concepts that would profoundly shape Thai civilization. Archaeological evidence shows fortified settlements and organized trade, suggesting the emergence of small kingdoms competing for control of fertile river valleys. These proto-Thai groups began migrating southward from southern China, gradually settling in areas that would become central Thailand.

items.label: Dvaravati Period
items.summary: Mon kingdoms ruled central Thailand, building the region's first significant urban centers and spreading Buddhism.
items.text: The Mon people, speaking an Austroasiatic language, established the Dvaravati confederation of city-states across central Thailand, with major centers at Nakhon Pathom, Lopburi, and U Thong. They were prolific Buddhist builders, constructing the earliest stupas and temples that established Thailand as a Theravada Buddhist heartland. The Mons developed an early writing system, minted coins, and created a distinctive artistic style blending Indian and local elements. Their decline began as Khmer power expanded from Cambodia, gradually absorbing the western Khmer territories while the northern regions remained beyond their reach.

items.label: Khmer Expansion
items.summary: The Khmer Empire extended its power across northeastern and eastern Thailand, building temples and administrative systems.
items.text: The Angkor Empire at its height controlled vast territories in present-day Thailand, constructing impressive stone temples like those at Phimai and Phnom Rung in the Isaan region. Khmer kings imposed their Hinduized court culture and Sanskrit administrative practices, though Theravada Buddhism persisted among the common people. The Khmer managed irrigation systems and collected taxes, establishing organized governance across conquered territories. However, their hold remained incomplete and gradually weakened as northern and western regions remained independent and Thai migration accelerated southward.

items.label: Rise of the Thai
items.summary: Thai peoples migrated southward and established independent kingdoms, overthrowing Khmer and Mon overlords.
items.text: Thai-speaking peoples, originally from southern China, accelerated their southward migration, establishing settlements in northern and central Thailand beyond Khmer control. The key turning point came at the Battle of Bang Yang in 1287, when Thai forces defeated the Khmer and established independence. Two major Thai kingdoms emerged: Sukhothai in the north and Lanna in the mountainous northwest, each claiming to be the true Thai heartland. The Sukhothai kingdom under King Ramkhamhaeng the Great became the model of early Thai civilization, creating the Thai alphabet and spreading Theravada Buddhism more widely.

items.label: Sukhothai Zenith and Ayutthaya Rise
items.summary: Sukhothai's golden age inspired the arts while a new super-power, Ayutthaya, rose in the central plains.
items.text: Sukhothai reached its cultural peak under the Ramkhamhaeng dynasty, producing exquisite bronze statues, elegant ceramic ware, and establishing the Thai temple form that endures today. Meanwhile, in 1351, Prince U Thong founded Ayutthaya at the confluence of three rivers, deliberately positioned to rival and eventually surpass Sukhothai as a trading power. Ayutthaya's strategic location attracted Chinese, Arab, Japanese, and European merchants, making it one of the world's largest and most cosmopolitan cities by the 17th century. As Ayutthaya's wealth and military strength grew, Sukhothai gradually declined, eventually becoming a vassal state and then a forgotten ruin.

items.label: Ayutthaya Dominance
items.summary: Ayutthaya became one of Asia's greatest powers, ruling most of present-day Thailand for three centuries.
items.text: For over three centuries, Ayutthaya dominated mainland Southeast Asia, controlling trade routes and incorporating neighboring territories through both conquest and diplomacy. The kingdom developed an elaborate bureaucracy centered on an absolute monarchy influenced by Hindu, Buddhist, and later European concepts, while maintaining strong cultural identity through Buddhism. Ayutthaya's wealth attracted international trade, leading European powers to establish factories and diplomatic missions that left detailed accounts of the kingdom's splendor and sophistication. The city became renowned for its hundreds of temples, royal palaces, and cosmopolitan atmosphere, where multiple religions coexisted and foreign merchants lived in segregated districts.

items.label: European Encounters
items.summary: European traders arrived and established significant commercial and political influence at the Ayutthaya court.
items.text: Portuguese, Dutch, English, and French merchants and diplomats arrived in Ayutthaya, seeking spices, exotic goods, and influence in Southeast Asian trade networks. King Narai (r. 1656-1688) actively engaged with the West, receiving embassies and granting trade privileges, particularly to the French who sought to convert the kingdom to Catholicism. The Greek adventurer Phaulkon rose to power as a minister and favorite of King Narai, negotiating foreign relations and amassing wealth that sparked jealousy among Thai nobles. However, upon Narai's death, a nationalist reaction led to Phaulkon's execution and the expulsion of most foreign traders, causing European interest in Siam to cool considerably.

items.label: Burmese Conquest and the Chakri Founding
items.summary: Burma destroyed Ayutthaya, but Thai forces quickly recovered and established the Chakri dynasty that rules today.
items.text: Burmese armies invaded and destroyed Ayutthaya in 1767, massacring thousands and ending the kingdom after 417 years of rule, with survivors scattered and the capital city burned. However, Thai resistance soon coalesced around General Taksin, who led the surviving forces from Thonburi and quickly rebuilt Thai military power, consolidating control over most Thai territories within a decade. After Taksin became increasingly erratic and was executed in 1782, his deputy General Chakri took power and established the current Chakri dynasty, moving the capital to Bangkok across the river. The new Bangkok kingdom maintained Taksin's military legacy while restoring more orthodox governance and religious observance, establishing a continuity that continues to the present day.

items.label: Chakri Dynasty Consolidation
items.summary: The Chakri kings modernized Thailand and preserved its independence while European powers colonized neighboring territories.
items.text: Kings Rama I through Rama V skillfully navigated the era of European colonization, gradually modernizing the kingdom's military, administration, and infrastructure while keeping Thailand independent. Rama IV and Rama V were particularly successful diplomats, ceding border territories to Britain and France to create buffer zones and demonstrating enough political sophistication to avoid colonization. They implemented administrative reforms, built railways and roads, established schools, and adopted European military technology while maintaining Thai cultural and Buddhist identity. Bangkok grew into a major regional power and trading hub, with increasing numbers of Chinese immigrants and international merchants establishing businesses in the city.

items.label: Absolute Monarchy to Constitutional Monarchy
items.summary: A bloodless revolution ended absolute monarchy and introduced constitutional rule, beginning Thailand's turbulent modern period.
items.text: In 1932, a coalition of military officers, bureaucrats, and intellectuals executed a peaceful coup that transformed Thailand from absolute to constitutional monarchy, establishing the world's first written constitution for a Southeast Asian nation. However, the transition proved chaotic, with multiple constitutions, coups, and counter-coups destabilizing the political system, particularly during World War II when Thailand initially allied with Japan then switched to the Allies. King Rama VIII died mysteriously in 1946, and his younger brother became King Rama IX (Bhumibol), whose seven-decade reign would become the world's longest-serving monarch. The post-war period saw Thailand struggling to establish political stability amid Cold War tensions and repeated cycles of democratic and military rule.

items.label: Modern Thailand
items.summary: Thailand endured decades of political instability punctuated by military coups, economic development, and cultural continuity centered on Buddhism and the monarchy.
items.text: The reign of King Bhumibol (Rama IX, 1946-2016) saw Thailand transform from an agrarian economy to a middle-income nation with significant industrial and tourism sectors, while repeatedly cycling through democratic and authoritarian periods. The Vietnam War era brought American military bases and substantial economic input, while Thailand's strategic location and cultural attractions made it a hub for Southeast Asian tourism and business. Thai culture, particularly Theravada Buddhism, remained a unifying force despite political volatility, with the monarchy serving as a symbol of national continuity and dignity. Since King Maha Vajiralongkorn's accession in 2016, Thailand has faced ongoing political tensions between traditionalists and reform movements, with the military intervening in 2014 and continuing constitutional struggles shaping the nation's trajectory into the 21st century.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Thailand — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Iced coffee is essential
items.text: Thai iced coffee (oliang) is thick, sweet, and served over ice—it's the national caffeine fix. Ask for 'iced coffee sai nam khao' if you want less condensed milk, or 'sai nam chuam' for lighter versions.

items.label: Oliang
items.text: Brewed from dark roasted beans mixed with sesame and corn, oliang has a distinctive nutty flavor that defines Thai café culture. It's cheap (20-30 baht) and available everywhere from street carts to upscale coffee shops.

items.label: Third-wave cafés in Bangkok
items.text: Areas like Ari, Thonglor, and Chinatown host excellent specialty coffee shops with single-origin beans and skilled baristas—expect 60-100 baht for quality espresso drinks. These spots are popular with young Thai professionals and offer reliable WiFi.

items.label: Condensed milk is the default
items.text: Thai coffee uses sweetened condensed milk as standard, making it very sweet. Specify 'mai sai nam chuam' (no condensed milk) or 'nam chuam nit noi' (just a little) if you prefer less sugar.

items.label: Morning markets have café stalls
items.text: Visit a floating market or street market early for fresh oliang served by vendors with generations of recipe experience. The quality often exceeds café chains and costs half the price.

items.label: Spice levels matter—communicate clearly
items.text: Thai food is customizable by heat: ask for 'mai pet' (not spicy), 'pet nit noi' (a little spicy), or 'pet' (normal spicy). Tourist restaurants often tone it down automatically; local spots may still deliver intense heat.

items.label: Point at other tables
items.text: In casual Thai restaurants without English menus, simply point at dishes from other diners' tables or show pictures on your phone. Thais appreciate this direct approach and the staff will explain what you're ordering.

items.label: Rice is separate and costs extra
items.text: Curries and stir-fries don't come with rice—you order it separately (about 10-15 baht). 'Khao' means rice; specify 'khao jasmine' for jasmine rice or 'khao sticky' for sticky rice.

items.label: Ask about daily specials
items.text: Lunch stalls (open 10am-2pm) offer 'set menu' combos with rice, protein, and soup for 30-50 baht. These change daily and represent the best value for authentic food.

items.label: Watch for tourist-trap symbols
items.text: Avoid restaurants near major attractions with laminated menus featuring photos and inflated prices (300+ baht for basic pad thai). Look for busy local spots with Thai-language menus and tables of office workers at lunch.

items.label: Pad Thai
items.text: Stir-fried rice noodles with tamarind, fish sauce, dried shrimp, and egg—Thailand's unofficial national dish. Get it from a street vendor (30-40 baht) rather than restaurants, where it's often oversweetened for tourists.

items.label: Som Tam (Green Papaya Salad)
items.text: Unripe papaya pounded with lime, fish sauce, chilies, and long beans—spicy, sour, and addictive. The preparation is theatrical; watch the vendor crush it in a mortar and specify your heat level.

items.label: Gaeng Massaman
items.text: A mild, rich curry with beef, potatoes, peanuts, and warm spices like cardamom and cinnamon. It's a great entry point for curry-hesitant travelers and pairs perfectly with jasmine rice.

items.label: Larb (Laap)
items.text: Minced meat salad from Northeast Thailand, seasoned with lime, fish sauce, and toasted rice powder. Order with 'sai gaeng' (with gravy) for a gentler version, or go traditional for a drier, more intense bowl.

items.label: Khao Soi (Northern curry noodle soup)
items.text: Egg noodles in a turmeric-coconut curry broth with meat, topped with crispy noodles and pickled vegetables—a Chiang Mai specialty. It's best eaten at dedicated khao soi stalls in the morning.

items.label: Satay (Sate)
items.text: Grilled meat skewers with peanut sauce, lime, and cucumber—a beloved street snack and appetizer. Quality varies wildly; seek stalls with high turnover and watch the meat being grilled fresh.

items.label: Street food stalls & night markets
items.text: Bangkok's Yaowarat (Chinatown) and Chatuchak Weekend Market, or Chiang Mai's Saturday/Sunday walking streets, offer the most authentic and affordable meals (20-60 baht). Go at peak hours when food is freshest.

items.label: Rice-and-curry shops (Khao Gaeng)
items.text: These humble lunch-only spots display 10-15 curries and stir-fried dishes in metal pots; you pick three items plus rice for 40-50 baht. Popular with office workers and locals seeking quick, real food.

items.label: Floating & morning markets
items.text: Damnoen Saduak (Floating Market) is touristy but real; head to Amphawa or Chumphon floating markets for fewer foreigners and fresher food. Morning markets (talad sod) open before 10am with produce, prepared dishes, and noodle vendors.

items.label: Seafood restaurants in coastal towns
items.text: In Phuket or Krabi, beachfront seafood spots offer grilled fish, prawns, and clams by weight (150-300 baht per 100g). Negotiate price first, verify the weight on return, and eat at lunch for fresher catch and lower prices.

items.label: Michelin-listed street food (Bangkok)
items.text: Several Bangkok street vendors hold Michelin stars; Nai Muan (boat noodles) and Jay Fai (Thai omelette) are famous but expect queues. These represent the pinnacle of street food but have become tourist attractions.

items.label: Eat where the locals eat
items.text: If a restaurant is full of Thai families and office workers, the food and prices are usually excellent. Empty restaurants near tourist areas are a warning sign.

items.label: Thai iced tea (Cha Yen)
items.text: Sweet, creamy orange-hued tea served iced—made with condensed milk and evaporated milk. It's Thailand's comfort beverage and costs 15-30 baht at any café or street stall.

items.label: Fresh fruit shakes & sugarcane juice
items.text: Thai street vendors blend fresh mango, papaya, or watermelon with ice into refreshing drinks (20-40 baht). Sugarcane juice is pressed fresh and incredibly sweet—sometimes mixed with lemon and salt.

items.label: Bia Thai (Local beer)
items.text: Brands like Chang, Leo, and Singha are light lagers served ice-cold in 330ml bottles (25-40 baht at street level, 60-150 baht in bars). Thai beer is best enjoyed cold and with food due to its light flavor.

items.label: Water bottles are essential
items.text: Buy a reusable bottle and refill at 7-Elevens (5 baht) or ask restaurants for free water ('nam plao khrap'). Tap water isn't safe for travelers; ice in drinks is usually made from filtered water but verify if concerned.

items.label: Respect alcohol sales restrictions
items.text: Thailand prohibits alcohol sales 2pm-5pm and after 11pm in most areas. Night markets and clubs operate after 11pm but convenience stores shut down. Plan accordingly, especially in smaller towns.

items.label: Thai whiskey cocktails (Mekhong)
items.text: Mekhong whiskey, a local spirit, is mixed into Thais Fizz (soda, lime) or enjoyed neat. It's cheap and smooth but deceptively strong; go slowly or stick with beer.

items.label: Respect the Royal Family
items.text: Never disrespect images or mention of the King—this is a serious offense. Dining establishments, temples, and public spaces display royal portraits; treat them with reverence.

items.label: Use utensils correctly
items.text: Use a spoon in your right hand as your primary eating utensil and a fork in your left to push food onto the spoon. Chopsticks are only for noodle dishes; avoid them for rice curries.

items.label: Share dishes and order family-style
items.text: Thai meals are communal; order multiple dishes (4-5 for a group of 4) and share from the center. Expect scoops of rice per person, and everyone eats from shared curry, salad, and stir-fry bowls.

items.label: Tipping is not obligatory but appreciated
items.text: There's no tipping culture in Thailand; rounding up to the nearest 10 baht or leaving 10% at upscale restaurants is kind but uncommon. Service charges are sometimes added to bills at fancier establishments.

items.label: Don't touch food with your left hand
items.text: The left hand is considered unclean in Thai culture; always eat and pass dishes with your right hand. This applies even if you're left-handed.

items.label: Start with mild dishes
items.text: If dining with Thais, let them guide the spice level and order progression. Never assume a dish is mild based on appearance; ask 'pet roi?' (is it spicy?) or 'sai pet nit noi' (make it a little spicy).

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Thailand.

items.label: State Railway of Thailand (SRT)
items.text: Book long-distance trains through the official SRT website or at major stations. Overnight sleeper trains to Chiang Mai, Ubon Ratchathani, and Surat Thani are popular and often full during peak season, so book 30 days in advance if possible.

items.label: Comfort classes
items.text: First-class sleeper cabins offer privacy; second-class is cheaper with shared bunks; third-class has bench seats. Train travel is slower than buses but more comfortable for long distances and a unique experience.
Trains frequently run 30 minutes to 2 hours late due to infrastructure and safety checks. Do not plan tight connections or same-day onward travel immediately after arrival.

items.label: Station safety
items.text: Bangkok's Hua Lamphong and Krung Thep Aphiwat stations are busy; watch bags in crowds and avoid carrying excessive cash. Use the left luggage service if storing items.

sections.title: City Metro & Buses [รถเมล์]

items.label: Bangkok BTS and MRT
items.text: The BTS Skytrain and MRT Subway are fast, safe, and air-conditioned. Buy a Rabbit Card (stored-value) or single journey tokens at machines; Rabbit Card is reloadable and works on most Bangkok transit.

items.label: Local buses
items.text: Cheap buses (5–15 baht) cover extensive routes but are crowded during rush hours and lack English signage. Use Google Maps or the ViaBus app to navigate routes; tell the conductor your destination or they will alert you at your stop.
Bus strikes occur occasionally in Bangkok and regional cities (common during political unrest). Check local news before relying on buses for time-sensitive trips; have a backup plan.

items.label: Provincial minibuses
items.text: Red or blue shared minibuses run fixed routes in cities like Chiang Mai and Phuket for 10–20 baht. Hail them on the street or ask your hotel; they depart when full, not on schedule.

sections.title: Taxis & Ride Apps [แท็กซี่]

items.label: Metered taxis
items.text: Bangkok metered taxis are relatively cheap and safe; ensure the meter is on before departing. Flag taxis with a lit rooftop light; pink taxis are woman-driver only and equally reliable.
Avoid unmarked taxis and negotiate fares beforehand—some drivers will refuse the meter or overcharge tourists. At major tourist areas, insist on the meter or use Grab instead.

items.label: Grab and Bolt apps
items.text: Use Grab (most popular in Thailand) or Bolt for reliable, app-booked rides with transparent pricing and ratings. Fares are slightly higher than metered taxis but safer; both apps work nationwide.

items.label: Tuk-tuks and songthaews
items.text: Tuk-tuks (three-wheelers) are iconic but expensive for tourists; negotiate price beforehand. Songthaews (shared red trucks) are cheaper and run set routes in many cities.
Tourist scams include drivers claiming your destination is closed and offering to take you shopping instead. Politely decline and ask to be taken to your original address; use Grab if uncertain.

items.label: International Driving Permit
items.text: Obtain an International Driving Permit (IDP) from your home country before arrival; Thailand technically requires it alongside your home license. Car rental agencies often accept just your home license, but IDP is legally safer.

Road conditions and driving behavior are chaotic; speed limits are often ignored, lane discipline is poor, and accidents are common. Drive defensively, use headlights day and night, and avoid driving at night outside cities.

Motorbike accidents are the leading cause of tourist deaths in Thailand. Wear a helmet (legally required), avoid riding under the influence, and consider hiring a driver instead if unfamiliar with conditions.

items.label: Fuel and tolls
items.text: Fuel is cheap; most stations accept cash and cards. Motorways (highways 7 and 9) charge tolls (50–250 baht); most rental cars have toll passes, but confirm with the rental company.
Police traffic stops may result in on-the-spot fines (bribery expected for minor violations). Keep documents visible, remain polite, and verify fines are legitimate; ask for a receipt if paying.

items.label: Suvarnabhumi and Don Mueang
items.text: Bangkok has two major airports: Suvarnabhumi (BKK, international) and Don Mueang (DMK, domestic and budget). Plan for 2–3 hours domestic, 3–4 hours international; traffic to/from airports is often heavy.

items.label: Airport transport
items.text: Use the Airport Rail Link (express 15 min, local 30 min) from Suvarnabhumi to central Bangkok, or Grab/metered taxi. Don Mueang connects via bus or taxi; avoid unmarked airport taxis.
Baggage handling and security are slow during peak hours. Arrive 2–3 hours early, especially for international flights, and do not pack valuables in checked luggage.

items.label: Regional airports
items.text: Phuket, Chiang Mai, and Krabi have international terminals but limited facilities. Domestic flights are cheap (often 500–1500 baht) and faster than buses or trains for long distances.

items.label: Island ferries
items.text: Ferries connect Phuket, Krabi, and Phang Nga to islands (Phi Phi, Similan, Railay). Book via Bukirályzat, Tigerline, or direct at piers; departures depend on sea conditions and weather.
Ferry schedules are irregular; boats may be delayed or cancelled in monsoon season (May–Oct on the west coast, Oct–Jan on the east). Always confirm departures the day before and have flexible plans.

items.label: Long-tail boats and speedboats
items.text: Charter private boats for island tours or multiple-island trips; negotiate prices beforehand. Speedboats are faster but pricier; long-tail boats are scenic and traditional.
Life jackets are rarely provided; request one from the operator or bring your own. Boats are unregulated; check that the engine and steering are in working condition before boarding.

items.label: River ferries
items.text: Bangkok's Chao Phraya Express ferries are cheap, scenic, and a tourist experience; use the Chao Phraya Card (reloadable) or buy single tickets at docks.

items.label: Train booking
items.text: Book Thai trains at www.railway.co.th or via 12go.asia; buy in person at stations for same-day travel. Advance bookings (30 days) secure better seats and lower prices during peak season.

items.label: Tourist passes
items.text: Bangkok's BTS/MRT Rabbit Card eliminates repeated queuing and offers small discounts. One Pass or Daily Pass options exist but are cost-effective only for frequent journeys.
Counterfeit or non-rechargeable cards circulate; buy only from official MRT/BTS vending machines and ticket windows. Keep receipts to verify card authenticity.

items.label: Bus tickets
items.text: Provincial buses (overnight to Chiang Mai, Phuket) are booked at bus stations or via Buikalyzat/12go.asia. Book at least 1–2 days ahead during holidays; prices vary by comfort class.
Refund policies are strict; cancellations often forfeit 10–50% of the fare. Always read cancellation terms before purchasing, and keep tickets as proof.

sections.title: Connectivity & SIM [ซิมการ์ด]

items.label: Local SIM cards
items.text: Buy a SIM card from AIS, DTAC, or True Move at airports, convenience stores, or phone shops. Prepaid plans are cheap (99–299 baht for data + calls); activation requires a passport copy and Thai phone number registration.

items.label: Data plans
items.text: 4G LTE coverage is excellent in cities and most towns. Tourist SIM packages offer 1–30 days of unlimited or large data pools; top up at 7-Eleven or directly from operators.
Network outages and throttling occur during peak hours or public holidays; avoid relying solely on mobile data for time-sensitive bookings. Bring a power bank and know your hotel's WiFi.

items.label: International roaming
items.text: If keeping your home SIM, enable airplane mode and use WiFi at hotels, cafes, and malls. Roaming charges are high; local SIM is cheaper for frequent data use.
Scams include overpriced tourist SIM cards at hotels; buy directly from operators instead. Ensure your SIM is unlocked before travel to avoid compatibility issues.
